content:
So I was following the quick start for compiling the LLVM toolchain with the kenrel and I got this error. 

─redhawk🎉Mythra─ ~/code/linux/rust-for-linux
 └$ make LLVM=1 -j16
  CALL    scripts/checksyscalls.sh
  DESCEND objtool
  INSTALL libsubcmd_headers
  DESCEND bpf/resolve_btfids
  INSTALL libsubcmd_headers
  VDSO2C  arch/x86/entry/vdso/vdso-image-64.c
  CC      arch/x86/entry/vdso/vdso-image-64.o
  CC      arch/x86/entry/vdso/vdso32/vclock_gettime.o
error: option 'cf-protection=branch' cannot be specified on this target
1 error generated.
make[5]: *** [scripts/Makefile.build:243: arch/x86/entry/vdso/vdso32/vclock_gettime.o] Error 1
make[4]: *** [scripts/Makefile.build:481: arch/x86/entry/vdso] Error 2
make[3]: *** [scripts/Makefile.build:481: arch/x86/entry] Error 2
make[2]: *** [scripts/Makefile.build:481: arch/x86] Error 2
make[1]: *** [/home/redhawk/code/linux/rust-for-linux/Makefile:1911: .] Error 2
make: *** [Makefile:234: __sub-make] Error 2

I've fixed the other errors before but I can't find anything online about this error relating to the kernel since llvm is used so widely.
Can you share which git revision, i.e. branch, tag, commit, of the kernel and which version of Clang are you using?
If you want to temporarily disable IBT which includes CF protection you can set CONFIG_X86_KERNEL_IBT to n in menuconfig.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Can you share which git revision, i.e. branch, tag, commit, of the kernel and which version of Clang are you using?

this commit 

commit 711cbfc717650532624ca9f56fbaf191bed56e67 (HEAD -> rust-next, origin/rust-next, origin/HEAD)
Author: Viresh Kumar <viresh.kumar@linaro.org>
Date:   Tue Dec 12 13:13:48 2023 +0530

    docs: rust: Clarify that 'rustup override' applies to build directory

    'rustup override' is required to be set for the build directory and not
    necessarily the kernel source tree (unless the build directory is its
    subdir).

    Clarify the same in the Quick Start guide.

    Signed-off-by: Viresh Kumar <viresh.kumar@linaro.org>
    Reviewed-by: Benno Lossin <benno.lossin@proton.me>
    Reviewed-by: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>
    Originally-pointed-out-by: Masahiro Yamada <masahiroy@kernel.org>
    Link: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commit/f2238e7
    Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/e2b943eca92abebbf035447b3569f09a7176c770.1702366951.git.viresh.kumar@linaro.org
    [ Reworded and fixed quotes for `--path` and `set`. ]
    Signed-off-by: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>

and this version of clang

clang version 17.0.6
Target: x86_64-suse-linux
Thread model: posix
InstalledDir: /usr/bin

Hmmm, you are on rust-next, targeting and using a version of Clang that supports CF protection, atm I don't know what produces your issue exactly, I can only recommend you to set CONFIG_X86_KERNEL_IBT to n

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Hmmm, you are on rust-next, targeting and using a version of Clang that supports CF protection, atm I don't know what produces your issue exactly, I can only recommend you to set CONFIG_X86_KERNEL_IBT to n

that fixed it, I would have never have figured this out on my own. If Im just building the kernel and not booting it should I just create a blank config?

Unless you're testing something so specific that you'd need a blank config, no, if you want to build the kernel only I'd recommend to either use your distro config and tweak it as you like it or use one of the template configs inside the .github/workflows folder.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Unless you're testing something so specific that you'd need a blank config, no, if you want to build the kernel only I'd recommend to either use your distro config and tweak it as you like it or use one of the template configs inside the .github/workflows folder.

Okay, I wasn't sure. Because I don't want a situtation where I have to take time out of my day to recompile the kernel because I need a new driver I excluded from my config because I wanted faster compile times.
Then the templates at .github/workflows folder will be the way to go, they contain the minimal stuff you might need for a test build.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Then the templates at .github/workflows folder will be the way to go, they contain the minimal stuff you might need to do a test build.

What exactly does it contain to save me compile time vs my distro's config?
Your distro config will enable bunch of drivers that you'll not even use while testing. That's what's most of the time spent building the kernel.
The kernel with a few modules take much less than 10 minutes to build.
And after it if you do only some changes only their sources will be compiled.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

And after it if you do only some changes only their sources will be compiled.

im not sure this actually enables the rust bindings to compile, i commented out a line to cause a syntax error and the compiler doesn't error and it seems like rustc is never invoked.

Carson Range said:

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

And after it if you do only some changes only their sources will be compiled.

im not sure this actually enables the rust bindings to compile, i commented out a line to cause a syntax error and the compiler doesn't error and it seems like rustc is never invoked.

so I did get it working with the config hidden in the github actions, however of course it didn't work. But at least rust code is getting compiled!
traceback.txt
what config are you using?

Ariel Miculas said:

what config are you using?

.config
it should be based off the ci config hosted on github, and then all the new options are added as defaults.
it wasn't me after all! https://github.com/ClangBuiltLinux/linux/issues/1984

Carson Range said:

it wasn't me after all! https://github.com/ClangBuiltLinux/linux/issues/1984

but now im wondering what I should do, unless my next step should be to recompile the entire toolchain?

Carson Range said:

Carson Range said:

it wasn't me after all! https://github.com/ClangBuiltLinux/linux/issues/1984

but now im wondering what I should do, unless my next step should be to recompile the entire toolchain?

I got LLVM16 and clang16 working

So I started on my first patch thanks to Wedson and tired to build it to get started and it seems bindgen has stopped working between the last time I posted this and then. I'm unsure what to do because using clang16 will give me this error

 └$ make LLVM=1 rustavailable
***
*** libclang (used by the Rust bindings generator 'bindgen')
*** version does not match Clang's. This may be a problem.
***   libclang version: 17.0.6
***   Clang version:    16.0.6
***
***
*** Please see Documentation/rust/quick-start.rst for details
*** on how to set up the Rust support.
***
Rust is available!

and using clang17 will cause clang to crash. I'm on opensuse tumbleweed and it seems distrobox is also broken currently. I'm at a lost of what to do.

That is not an error, it's simply a warning, Rust is available indeed but the version mismatch might result in failure while compiling. You can build the kernel but YMMV. That bindgen binary has been built against libclang 17.0.6 but if you want for the warning to disappear or you happen to encounter a compilation error then you'll have to rebuild bindgen against libclang 16.0.6.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

That is not an error, it's simply a warning, Rust is available indeed but the version mismatch might result in failure while compiling. You can build the kernel but YMMV. That bindgen binary has been built against libclang 17.0.6 but if you want for the warning to disappear or you happen to encounter a compilation error then you'll have to rebuild bindgen against libclang 16.0.6.

I know its a warning but it doesn't actually compile any of the rust code, I can create basic syntax errors to show it doesn't compile the rust parts of the code. I was thinking about creating a dockerfile to create a env that I can actually compile the kernel in with rust since it seems distrobox doesn't work either.

Also is anyone here using clang 17.0.6? that version crashes for me when I compile the kernel, so I'm unsure if it's just me. It seems that opensuse has the most recent package of clang and I'm bleeding on the bleeding edge.
How did you manage to get bindgen built against libclang 17? What version of bindgen are you using?
You can get the required version of bindgen by scripts/min-tool-version.sh bindgen

Andreas Hindborg said:

How did you manage to get bindgen built against libclang 17? What version of bindgen are you using?

i built bindgen a while ago and I ran the command again today and it said it was upto date.

What I do is this:

rustup path="linux":
    #!/bin/bash
    version="$({{path}}/scripts/min-tool-version.sh bindgen)"
    rustc_version="$({{path}}/scripts/min-tool-version.sh rustc)"
    mkdir -p {{path}}-build
    rustup override set --path={{path}}-build $rustc_version
    cargo install bindgen-cli --locked --version "$version" --root $(pwd)/bindgen-"$version"
    rustup component add --toolchain=$rustc_version rust-src
So I install a local version of bindgen specific to the kernel sources

I was just following the getting started rust guide

https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/rust/quick-start.html
It should link to the default version of libclang usged by the clang command

Andreas Hindborg said:

It should link to the default version of libclang usged by the clang command

what version of clang are you using?
Clang 16

Andreas Hindborg said:

Clang 16

so your entire toolchain is clang and llvm 16?
Yes.
and i assume rust actually compiles on your end
and isn't ignored in the compiling process
Absolutely :)
If clang 16 is not the default you can pass LLVM=-16 I think
That would pick clang-16 for the kernel build instead of just clang
and that sets the clang version? I thought LLVM= was just a boolean?

The LLVM= argument
------------------

LLVM has substitutes for GNU binutils utilities. They can be enabled
individually. The full list of supported make variables::

    make CC=clang LD=ld.lld AR=llvm-ar NM=llvm-nm STRIP=llvm-strip \
      OBJCOPY=llvm-objcopy OBJDUMP=llvm-objdump READELF=llvm-readelf \
      HOSTCC=clang HOSTCXX=clang++ HOSTAR=llvm-ar HOSTLD=ld.lld

``LLVM=1`` expands to the above.

If your LLVM tools are not available in your PATH, you can supply their
location using the LLVM variable with a trailing slash::

    make LLVM=/path/to/llvm/

which will use ``/path/to/llvm/clang``, ``/path/to/llvm/ld.lld``, etc. The
following may also be used::

    PATH=/path/to/llvm:$PATH make LLVM=1

If your LLVM tools have a version suffix and you want to test with that
explicit version rather than the unsuffixed executables like ``LLVM=1``, you
can pass the suffix using the ``LLVM`` variable::

    make LLVM=-14

which will use ``clang-14``, ``ld.lld-14``, etc.

To support combinations of out of tree paths with version suffixes, we
recommend::

    PATH=/path/to/llvm/:$PATH make LLVM=-14

``LLVM=0`` is not the same as omitting ``LLVM`` altogether, it will behave like
``LLVM=1``. If you only wish to use certain LLVM utilities, use their
respective make variables.

The same value used for ``LLVM=`` should be set for each invocation of ``make``
if configuring and building via distinct commands. ``LLVM=`` should also be set
as an environment variable when running scripts that will eventually run
``make``.
It has a little bit of flexibility in selecting the toolchain

Andreas Hindborg said:

It has a little bit of flexibility in selecting the toolchain

Not as much as I need 

 └$ make LLVM=-16
  SYNC    include/config/auto.conf
  HOSTCC  scripts/basic/fixdep
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/conf.o
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/confdata.o
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/expr.o
  LEX     scripts/kconfig/lexer.lex.c
  YACC    scripts/kconfig/parser.tab.[ch]
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/lexer.lex.o
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/menu.o
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/parser.tab.o
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/preprocess.o
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/symbol.o
  HOSTCC  scripts/kconfig/util.o
  HOSTLD  scripts/kconfig/conf
scripts/Kconfig.include:40: linker 'ld.lld-16' not found
make[3]: *** [scripts/kconfig/Makefile:85: syncconfig] Error 1
make[2]: *** [Makefile:689: syncconfig] Error 2
make[1]: *** [/home/redhawk/code/linux/rust-for-linux/Makefile:790: include/config/auto.conf] Error 2
make[1]: *** [include/config/auto.conf] Deleting file 'include/generated/rustc_cfg'
make[1]: *** [include/config/auto.conf] Deleting file 'include/generated/autoconf.h'
make: *** [Makefile:240: __sub-make] Error 2

─redhawk🎉Mythra─ ~/code/linux/rust-for-linux
 └$ zypper info lld16
Loading repository data...
Reading installed packages...


Information for package lld16:
------------------------------
Repository     : Main Repository (OSS)
Name           : lld16
Version        : 16.0.6-3.1
Arch           : x86_64
Vendor         : openSUSE
Installed Size : 4.7 MiB
Installed      : Yes
Status         : up-to-date
Source package : llvm16-16.0.6-3.1.src
Upstream URL   : https://lld.llvm.org/
Summary        : Linker for Clang/LLVM
Description    :
    LLD is a linker from the LLVM project. That is a drop-in replacement for system linkers and runs much faster than them. It also provides features that are useful for toolchain developers.

I think I know whats happening, on opensuse it seems if you have clang 15 16 and 17 installed, clang -v will return clang17 and the others can be accessed using clang15 and clang16 I tried again using make LLVM=1 It seems to have compile, but I'll have to check to see if rust code compiles with it.
If it’s clang16 you should just pass 16 as LLVM, not -16

Andreas Hindborg said:

If it’s clang16 you should just pass 16 as LLVM, not -16

It did compile but It should have crashed at my addition to the rust code

// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0

//! String representations.

asdljnaskudliasdlhjaskld;ajksd;;;;

use alloc::alloc::AllocError;
use alloc::vec::Vec;
use core::fmt::{self, Write};
use core::ops::{self, Deref, Index};
Can I also ask about your config? If my mistake is somewhere in there I will never find it.
If it's not the config the only other guess I have is it's related to libclang being too updated

You can check if rust is enabled.

> grep CONFIG_RUST linux-build/.config
CONFIG_RUST_IS_AVAILABLE=y
CONFIG_RUST=y
CONFIG_RUSTC_VERSION_TEXT="rustc 1.73.0 (cc66ad468 2023-10-03)"
# CONFIG_RUST_DEBUG_ASSERTIONS is not set
CONFIG_RUST_OVERFLOW_CHECKS=y
# CONFIG_RUST_BUILD_ASSERT_ALLOW is not set

I usually configure like this:

CONFIG_64BIT=y
CONFIG_X86_64=y
CONFIG_X86=y

CONFIG_CC_OPTIMIZE_FOR_PERFORMANCE=y
CONFIG_SMP=y
CONFIG_KERNEL_XZ=y

CONFIG_SERIAL_8250_CONSOLE=y

and then run make olddefconfig

that's your entire config for rfl?
Yes. It expands quite a bit when you do the oldddefconfig thing
olddefconfig applies the default values for anything you did not set an option for
If you want something smaller, you can start with make allnoconfig and then merge in the fragment above and then do olddefconfig
I wrote a bit about how I do that here https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/291565-Help/topic/Build.20error.20on.20rust.20next.20when.20using.20minimal.20.2Econfig/near/419022785

Andreas Hindborg said:

Yes. It expands quite a bit when you do the oldddefconfig thing

I guess it was just my config was bad, I have no idea how that happened I should have copied it from the github actions for rfl. I highly suggest you add that config somewhere on the getting started if possible. But that fixed it! and now I have this dockerfile I wrote to build the a container for the kernel lol. If anyone needs it 

FROM rust:bookworm

WORKDIR /app
COPY . .

RUN apt update
RUN apt install -y build-essential bc git kmod cpio flex libncurses5-dev libelf-dev libssl-dev dwarves bison clang llvm lld

RUN rustup override set $(scripts/min-tool-version.sh rustc)
RUN rustup component add rust-src
RUN cargo install --locked --version $(scripts/min-tool-version.sh bindgen) bindgen-cli

People use all kinds of different systems with all kinds of different configurations, so it is not really feasible to add this particular piece. There is a rust config in the tree so you can make rust.config I think. I have not tried to use it though.

For instance, the config fragment I gave you now I use when I boot into qemu. But it does not work if the userland in the vm uses systemd. So if I want to boot into a debian userland, I have to add an extra fragment. If want to boot real hardware, I would add yet more config fragments.
There is no one size fits all for this.

Andreas Hindborg said:

For instance, the config fragment I gave you now I use when I boot into qemu. But it does not work if the userland in the vm uses systemd. So if I want to boot into a debian userland, I have to add an extra fragment. If want to boot real hardware, I would add yet more config fragments.

That's okay I don't plan on booting this kernel, at least not yet.
